mnemonics.title: Basis Rememberance
mnemonics.content: BASIS: BAsis = Set of Independent Vectors that Span. Remember: a basis needs INDEPENDENCE and SPANNING - both required!

mnemonics.title: Dimension Rule
mnemonics.content: DIMENSION: Count the vectors in any basis. All bases → Same Number! (Dim = cardinality of basis)

mnemonics.title: Linear Independence Test
mnemonics.content: LI Test: Write as matrix, row reduce. Free variables → dependent. Pivots → independent. Count pivots = dimension.

acronyms.expansion: Linearly Independent - no vector can be written as combo of others
acronyms.usage: Check if a set forms a basis by testing L.I.

acronyms.expansion: Reduced Row Echelon Form - used to find basis and dimension
acronyms.usage: Row reduce a matrix to RREF to identify pivot columns for basis

acronyms.expansion: Null Space of matrix A - solution to Ax=0
Column Space of matrix A - span of columns
acronyms.usage: Dimension of C(A) = rank of A

visualTips.visualization: Think of basis vectors as the axes of your coordinate system. In R², basis is like x and y axes; in R³, x, y, z axes. Any point (vector) is located using these axes.
Dimension = number of independent directions. A line (1D) has 1 direction, plane (2D) has 2, space (3D) has 3. More vectors don't increase dimension if dependent.
Think of spanning vectors as building blocks. With enough independent blocks, you can build ANY vector in the space - like LEGO bricks.
Dependent vectors are redundant - like having both 'north' and '2×north' in your set. One adds no new direction.

analogies.analogy: Just as street addresses need house number and street name (2 coordinates), every vector needs its 'address' - the basis coordinates.
A shadow of a 3D object on a wall is 2D - it lost one dimension. Similarly, a plane in R³ has dimension 2 because it's 'flattened'.
Independent vectors are like different directions on a compass. Dependent vectors point in the same/scaled direction - redundant information.
In a factory: rank = useful output dimensions, nullity = wasted/constrained dimensions. Total variables = rank + nullity.
